coredata.dc:title: Novel solution and solid-state emissive long-wavelength carbon dots for water sensing and white LED applications
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Weiying
Sun, Zishan
Fan, Jiaqi
Huang, Wenjing
Zhang, Jiahui
Song, Hongji
Zhou, Liya
Huang, Jinqing
Wu, Zhan-chao
Zhang, Xinguo
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) are among the most popular nanomaterials due to their remarkable fluorescent and electronic properties, as well as their good biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity. Currently, CDs with aggregation-induced emission (AIE) are relatively rare and have become a significant research hotspot. This is because most conventional CDs suffer from severe quenching in a solid state. Herein, novel CDs with both solution and solid-state emissions were obtained using a facile one-step hydrothermal synthesis. Specifically, the CDs exhibit yellow solvent-dependent fluorescence in solution state (λ em = 580 nm) and red AIE emission in solid state (λem = 640 nm). CDs powder is utilized as a red phosphor for light-emitting diode (LED). The resulting fabricated white LEDs (WLEDs) demonstrate good performance metrics, including a Color Rendering Index (CRI) of 89.5, Correlated Color Temperature (CCT) of 3876 K, and commission Internationale d’Éclairage (CIE) coordinates of (0.350, 0.331). Furthermore, the solvent-dependent phenomenon observed in AIE-CDs can be exploited to apply their solution as a highly sensitive fluorescence sensor for quantitatively detecting amounts of water in various organic solvents. The method offers both high accuracy and sensitivity, with R2 values varying from 0.853 to 0.994 and a low detection limit ranging from 0.296 to 3.23 % across a wide linear range. This inherent versatility makes the CDs suitable for a wide range of applications, including sensing and LED devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Aggregation-induced emission
Water sensing
LED phosphor

Jinqing Huang acknowledges the financial support from Science, Technology and Innovation Commission of Shenzhen Municipality (Project number JCYJ20180306174930894 )

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The TEM image of AIE-CDs. (b) The HRTEM image of AIE-CDs. (c) FTIR spectra of AIE-CDs and DTSA-CDs. (d) The wide scan XPS spectrum.

(a) Emission spectra of AIE-CDs with various solvents. (b) Fluorescence intensities of AIE-CDs in THF with different concentrations. (c) Excitation and emission and UV–Vis spectrum of AIE-CDs in THF. (d) PL mapping spectra of AIE-CDs in THF at different excitation wavelengths. (e)The lifetime value of AIE-CDs in THF. (f) Schematic of energy level and fluorescence decay of AIE-CDs solution with different electron donating ability.

(a) Excitation and emission spectra of AIE-CDs powder. (b) PL mapping spectra of AIE-CDs powder at different excitation wavelengths. (c) The lifetime value of AIE-CDs powder. (d) Schematic of energy level and fluorescence decay of AIE-CDs powder with different electron donating ability. (e) 3D PL mapping spectra of AIE-CDs powder in different temperature. (f) 3D PL mapping spectra of optical stability under UV irradiation.
(a) PL responses of AIE-CDs in DMSO toward different amounts of water (c) with the corresponding photographs at the excitation of 500 nm, and (b) the linear relationship of I/I0 to water content.
The fitting results of AIE-CDs for the water contents in different solvents. Fitting formula: y = a + bx.
(a, c) The electroluminescence spectra of the fabricated WLEDs and (b) the corresponding CIE coordinates along with (d) pictures of color-markers at daylight and WLEDs lighting.
Synthesis, solid/solution property and applications of AIE-CDs.
The absorption and emission data of CDs in different solvents.
The performance parameters of AIE-CDs used for water detection in organic solvents.

head.ce:title: Novel solution and solid-state emissive long-wavelength carbon dots for water sensing and white LED applications

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: 1 These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) are among the most popular nanomaterials due to their remarkable fluorescent and electronic properties, as well as their good biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity. Currently, CDs with aggregation-induced emission (AIE) are relatively rare and have become a significant research hotspot. This is because most conventional CDs suffer from severe quenching in a solid state. Herein, novel CDs with both solution and solid-state emissions were obtained using a facile one-step hydrothermal synthesis. Specifically, the CDs exhibit yellow solvent-dependent fluorescence in solution state (λ em = 580 nm) and red AIE emission in solid state (λem = 640 nm). CDs powder is utilized as a red phosphor for light-emitting diode (LED). The resulting fabricated white LEDs (WLEDs) demonstrate good performance metrics, including a Color Rendering Index (CRI) of 89.5, Correlated Color Temperature (CCT) of 3876 K, and commission Internationale d’Éclairage (CIE) coordinates of (0.350, 0.331). Furthermore, the solvent-dependent phenomenon observed in AIE-CDs can be exploited to apply their solution as a highly sensitive fluorescence sensor for quantitatively detecting amounts of water in various organic solvents. The method offers both high accuracy and sensitivity, with R2 values varying from 0.853 to 0.994 and a low detection limit ranging from 0.296 to 3.23 % across a wide linear range. This inherent versatility makes the CDs suitable for a wide range of applications, including sensing and LED devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to their remarkable fluorescent and electronic properties in the dispersed solution state, carbon dots (CDs) are among the most promising nano-materials for bioimaging, sensing, and optoelectronic devices [1–5]. Currently, most synthesized CDs exhibit high quantum yield (QY) in the blue/green luminescent spectral regions. This limitation hinders their applications in imaging, sensing, and electroluminescent devices [2,6,7]. In contrast, bright long-wavelength CDs (λ em > 600 nm) with emission in the red/near-infrared region are relatively lacking [8,9]. Besides, while the fluorescence of CDs in aqueous solutions has been extensively explored, research on solid state fluorescence (SSF) CDs is rare [10]. The majority of CDs suffer the aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) effect in the solid state [11–13]. To overcome the drawback of ACQ effect, the widely employed method is to incorporate CDs into appropriate solid matrices, such as silica xerogel, starch, and polymers [14–16]. Only a few reported CDs have managed to conquer the ACQ effect without the use of solid matrices. Thus, the development of novel matrix-free aggregation-induced emission (AIE) CDs is emerging as a hot topic in CDs research.

In the field of optoelectronics, organic light-emitting diodes (OLED) is predominantly employed for display technologies, such as televisions, mobile phone screens, and display panels, while light-emitting diodes (LED) is well-suited for a wide range of lighting applications, particularly in scenarios that demand high brightness, long lifespan, and low energy consumption [17–24]. Inorganic phosphors and quantum dots (QDs) are widely used as conversion components of WLED [20,25,26]. However, inorganic phosphors typically utilize rare-earth as emitting centers, which are scarce and require a high-temperature sintering process to activate their luminescence. QDs often contain heavy metals such as Pb, Cd and Hg, which are toxic and limit practical application [27]. Due to their facile synthesis, excellent luminescence, and low cytotoxicity, solid-state CDs could serve as conversion components in WLEDs [28]. Unfortunately, the general ACQ effect in conventional CDs has hindered the pursuit of high efficiency in phosphor-converted WLEDs (pc-WLEDs) [29,30]. Consequently, novel CDs with efficient AIE property are promising candidates for pc-WLEDs application. Meanwhile, water impurity is extremely undesirable in many organic and inorganic processes [31]. Meanwhile, water content detection is essential in drugs, chemicals, and food systems [32]. The standard methods require precision instruments and complex processing [33]. Therefore, effective and easily deployable water content detection sensors are needed. Due to their sensitivity, simplicity, fast response, and low cost, CDs solution with optical capabilities could be developed to detect water content in various organic solvents [34,35]. Thus, the development of CDs-based luminescent probes for water detection holds significant value.

In this study, a novel AIE-CDs were synthesized using hydrothermal method, with dithiosalicylic acid (DTSA) and polyetherimide (PEI) serving as carbon and nitrogen sources. Following a simple process, AIE-CDs solution and powder were obtained (Scheme 1 , SI 1.4). The CDs solution exhibits yellow dispersed fluorescence (λ em = 580 nm) and the solid state demonstrates bright red AIE (λ em = 640 nm), making them suitable for various applications. The prepared CDs powder with AIE properties exhibits a bright red emission, which could be utilized in the fabrication of WLEDs. Furthermore, the AIE-CDs solution shows a solvent-dependent phenomenon, where both emission intensity and peak wavelength could be tuned by varying of polarity. Thus, the AIE-CDs solution could be employed to detect water content in various organic solvents. This work demonstrates that our newly-developed AIE-CDs powder could serve as a red phosphor for pc-WLEDs, while its dispersed solution could function as a florescent indicator for quantifying water content in various organic solvents.

The characterizations of AIE-CDs were analyzed using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), X-rays diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier transforms infrared (FTIR), and nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image reveals that the particle size of AIE-CDs is approximately 4 nm (Fig. 1 a). HRTEM image (inset) clearly displays interspatial distances of 0.15 nm and 0.18 nm, corresponding to the (002) and (100) panes, respectively (Fig. 1b) [36–38]. The XRD patterns of AIE-CDs exhibit an unobvious peak at 2θ = 26◦, confirming interspatial distances of 0.15 nm. This finding indicates that AIE-CDs possess a graphite-like structure with good crystallinity. The peak near 41° further verifies an interspatial distance around 0.18 nm (Fig. S1). The XRD pattern depicts two peaks at 26° and 41°, which is corresponding to (002) and (100) planes of the graphitic structure.

Deuterium-labeled DMSO‑d 6 (CD3SOCD3) was used as the solvent in NMR spectroscopy (1H and 13C). Fig. S2 displays the 1H spectrum of AIE-CDs. Signals within the range of 7.77–8.42 ppm are assigned to –CH, originating from the aromatic rings of DTSA. Additionally, –OH is present at around 12.75 ppm. The 13C NMR spectrum of AIE-CDs (Fig. S3) reveals six chemical shift peaks at 125.40, 126.39, 128.54, 131.98, 133.67 and 139.33 ppm, which are attributed to the presence of aromatic ring. The signal at 168.03 ppm corresponds to carboxyl/amide groups. FTIR analysis (Fig. 1c) reveals the presence of functional groups, such as methylene (2872 cm−1), S-H (2518 cm−1), amide carbonyl (1682 cm−1), C-N (1589 cm−1), C = C (1462 cm−1), aromatic C-NH (1261 cm−1), C-O (1039 cm−1), C-S (741 cm−1) and S-S (552 cm−1). In contrast, CDs prepared using only DTSA as a precursor (DTSA-CDs) were also synthesized. FTIR analysis indicates that AIE-CDs possess an amino group (3073 cm−1), indicating the existence of PEI fragment as a nitrogen source and surface passivation agents in CDs. DTSA-CDs also contains a hydroxyl group (3426 cm−1). Following amination and carbonization, the hydrophilic groups disappear, resulting in an increase in hydrophobicity [39,40].

XPS analysis of AIE-CDs (Fig. 1d) reveals four peaks at 164.08, 285.54, 399.20, and 531.14 eV, attributed to S 2p, C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s, respectively. The atomic ratios of elements C, O, S and N are calculated as 72.38 %, 18.57 %, 7.06 %, and 1.99 %, respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1 s (Fig. S4a) exhibits C–C/C = C (285.6 eV) and C = O/C = N (283.6 eV) bonds [40]. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of O 1 s (Fig. S4b) displays two peaks at 532.4 and 530.9 eV, attributed to C = O and C-O [41]. The peaks (Fig. S4c) at 399.6 and 398.5 eV in the N 1 s suggest that nitrogen primarily exists in the forms of C-N and N–H, respectively [42]. The S 2p band (Fig. S4d) includes two peaks at 162.4 and 163.4 eV, assigned to S-C and S-S, respectively. All the results confirm the successful doping of N and S into AIE-CDs. Based on the aforementioned characterizations, functional groups are present on the surface of AIE-CDs, including C-S, C = N/C-N and OH/N–H. However, the presence of only a few amino and hydroxyl functional groups contribute to hydrophobicity and other properties [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of AIE-CDs solution and powder
ce:section.ce:para: AIE-CDs solution (THF) was chosen to investigate the optical properties in solvents, with the strongest fluorescence observed among them. The effect of the concentration of the AIE-CDs solution was also investigated, ranging from 1 mg/mL to 10 mg/mL. A red shift of the peak wavelength from 587 nm to 599 nm appears (Fig. 2b). As shown in Fig. 2c, UV–Vis absorption exhibits two peaks at 445 nm and 540 nm due to the π–π* transitions and n–π* transition, respectively. AIE-CDs solution exhibits a bright yellow emission at 595 nm upon 538 nm excitation. The QY of AIE-CDs solution in THF can be calculated as 3.93% (Fig. S5). The graphs of AIE-CDs solution is of yellow transparent at daylight, and the yellow fluorescence is also observed under 500 nm wavelength excitation (Fig. 2c inset). Additionally, the emission wavelength of AIE-CDs solution is excitation-dependent, showing a slight red shift from 583 to 595 nm when excited from 400 to 540 nm. This indicates the presence of side energy levels with an energy gap (Fig. 2d). The lifetime of AIE-CDs solution is evaluated to be 5.13 ns (Fig. 2e). In the dispersed state, the locations of the inherent main energy levels are specific to certain CDs, while the side energy levels vary with the surface states of CDs particles (Fig. 2f).

Meanwhile, the fluorescent spectra of AIE-CDs powder were examined. Under 565 nm excitation, AIE-CDs powder emits a bright red emission centered at 640 nm, confirming the successful preparation of AIE-CDs powder (Fig. 3 a). The absolute QY of AIE-CDs powder was measured and calculated to be 3.3 % (Fig. S6), which is similar to the reported QY of CDs with AIE property (5.96 % [43], 4.7 % [45], 5.89 % [46]). The emission wavelength of AIE-CDs is nearly independent of the excitation wavelength (Fig. 3b), likely originating from the intrinsic bandgap transitions within the conjugated π-domains and molecular fluorophores as fluorescent centers [47,48]. Time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) experiment demonstrates a lifetime of 10.24 ns for AIE-CDs powder fluorescence, which is irrespective of excitation wavelength (Fig. 3c). The lack of excitation-dependence could result from the integration or coupling of main energy levels and side energy levels when AIE-CDs aggregate (Fig. 3d). The interaction between main energy levels and side energy levels leads to the formation of integrated energy levels. Most electrons relax to the lowest vibration energy level (T 1) via intersystem crossing and return to the ground state (S 0) through red fluorescence, resulting in a longer lifetime (10.24 ns) and no excitation-dependence. The convincing mechanism of aggregate and luminous for AIE-CDs with DTSA-modified surface has been explored. The addition of water induces hydrophobic interactions and cluster formation, leading to the activation of red fluorescence and deactivation of yellow fluorescence. The presence of π-π* stacking interactions in graphitized cores is attributed to restricted intramolecular rotation around disulfide bonds, aligning with the AlE phenomenon [43]. Finally, AIE-CDs adopt a head-to-tail arrangement, driven by the restriction of intramolecular rotation, similar to axisymmetric heterocycles reported in AlE molecules [39,43,49–51]. Moreover, PEI also effect the AIE property in CDs. Abundant amino groups in PEI provide reactive sites for dehydration, supporting the formation of luminescence centers. What’s more, polymer backbones and branched chains restrict adjacent fluorescence centers in the solid state, preventing interparticle surface electron transitions and π–π* interactions of the graphitizing cores. Thus, matrix-free CDs can avoid ACQ and maintain efficient luminescence in the solid state [52]. To further support this point, CDs using only DTSA or PEI as a precursor were also prepared under the same condition. However, no obvious fluorescence is observed in CDs powder synthetized from the sole carbon source, indicating that the coexistence of DTSA and PEI as carbon and nitrogen source are critical for the AIE property of CDs.

In order to investigate the thermal stability and UV optical stability of our AIE-CDs powder, the PL spectra under varying temperatures and irradiation times were measured. As depicted in Fig. 3e and Fig. S7, AIE-CDs powder maintains over 50 % of its room temperature intensity at 423 K. As shown in Fig. 3f, the emission intensity remains constant even after exposure to intense UV radiation (wavelength: 365 nm, power: 12 W) up to 12 h. This observation confirms the exceptional optical stability exhibited by AIE-CDs.

The utilization of CDs for water content detection stems from its rapid response to polarity changes and solvent-dependent phenomenon, as previously reported [31–34]. Interactions between the surface groups of CDs and various solvent molecules significantly influence their fluorescent properties [35–37]. The presence of solvatochromic behavior, coupled with tunable emission, suggest a promising avenue for water content detection [38]. Building on the earlier discussion, AIE-CDs solution exhibits a polarity-sensitive and solvent-dependent nature, rendering it a potential luminescent sensor. To deepen our understanding, AIE-CDs were employed to detect water content in seven organic solvents, including DMSO, isopropanol, DMF, acetonitrile, acetone, 1,4-dioxane, and THF. Fig. 4 a illustrates the PL of AIE-CDs in water/DMSO mixed solution, revealing a distinct linear correlation with the 0 ∼ 100% range (Fig. 4b). Notably, there is a noticeable decline in the relative fluorescent intensity, shifting from 100% to 7.52% as the water content varies within the 0 to 100% range, indicating a potential correspondence with the Stern-Volmer equation: y = -0.00941 × + 0.982, R2 = 0.994, where y is I/I0 . In a more intuitive sense, when subjected to 500 nm excitation, the color transitions from a vivid yellow to a deeper hue when water content is increased from 0 to 50%. Additionally, the introduction of 50% to 100% water induces the formation of a red precipitate within the AIE-CDs solution. This transformation entails the shift from a homogeneous and transparent liquid to a turbid state (Fig. 4c). Therefore, AIE-CDs aggregates exhibit red SSF, while the monomers continue to emit a yellow emission.

In addition, the performance of AIE-CDs as a water detection tool extends to a diverse range of solvents, encompassing isopropanol, DMF, acetonitrile, acetone, 1,4-dioxane and THF. Fig. 5 shows the intricate relationship between fluorescence intensity and water content across these distinct solvents, revealing a notable linear response with R2 values ranging from 0.853 to 0.994, coupled with a discerning limit of detection (LOD) spanning from 0.30 to 3.2%. When contrasted with previously reported water sensing materials, which typically exhibit R2 values ranging from 0.908 to 0.999 and a detection range extending between 0.0 and 0.8% to 0–100% [43,53–55], AIE-CDs solution showcases remarkable sensitivity, showcasing an extensive linear range within seven dinstinct solvents: DMSO (0 ∼ 100%), isopropanol (0 ∼ 100%), DMF (0 ∼ 10/20 ∼ 70%), acetonitrile (10 ∼ 100%), acetone (0 ∼ 10/20 ∼ 100%), 1,4-dioxane (0 ∼ 10/20 ∼ 100%) and THF (0 ∼ 10/20 ∼ 80%) (Table 2 ).

As seen from Scheme 1, a homogeneous mixture of commercially available yellow-emitting YAG: Ce3+ phosphors and red-emitting AIE-CDs powder were uniformly dispersed within an epoxy resin silicone. This composite was then deposited onto InGaN blue chips, culminating in the fabrication of the pc-WLEDs device. The resulting electro-luminescence (EL) spectrum of the pc-WLEDs demonstrates a seamless and broad emission spanning the entire visible spectrum, yielding a luminous efficiency of 54.01 lm/W when operated under a forward current of 20 mA, as illustrated in Fig. 6 a. The commission Internationale d’Éclairage (CIE), correlated color temperature (CCT), and color rendering index (CRI), of the prepared WLEDs are found to be (0.350, 0.331), 3876 K, and 89.5, respectively (Fig. 6b). This combination of a low CCT and a noteworthy CRI value underscores the exceptional white light performance achieved by employing AIE-CDs as red phosphor. Furthermore, the EL spectra of the pc-WLEDs, spanning a forward drive current range of 20 to 200 mA, are shown in Fig. 6c. It is evident from these spectra that the performance of the pc-WLEDs exhibits improvement as the drive current is increased. This improvement is further quantified in Table S1, which summarizes the color stability data under varying forward bias currents. Notably, as the current increases from 20 to 200 mA, small changes are observed in both CRI and CIE values, attesting to the outstanding color stability intrinsic to the fabricated WLEDs. In a word, the versatile application potential of red-emitting AIE-CDs in WLEDs is clearly evident. Additionally, the illumination rendered by these WLEDs closely approximates that of natural daylight, reflecting a high degree of color accuracy (Fig. 6d). Thus, AIE-CDs emerge as a promising red phosphor candidate for advancing the performance of pc-WLEDs.

Herein, AIE-CDs with both solution and solid-state emission were synthesized using a hydrothermal method, employing raw materials DTSA and PEI, known for their aggregation-induced emission properties in solid or aggregated state. When dispersed in solvents like THF, CDs solution emits a yellow emission (λem = 580 nm) upon green excitation (λex = 500 nm). A bright AIE effect (λem = 640 nm, λex = 565 nm) is observed when CDs aggregate. This AIE behavior is attributed to the constraint imposed by disulfide bonds on the intramolecular rotation of surface groups, thus inducing AIE property. Additionally, PEI functions as nitrogen source within CDs. As the water content increases, the emission intensity of AIE-CDs solutions quenches due to the polarity-sensitive phenomenon. This property makes AIE-CDs apt for detecting water content in various organic solvents. Notably, the relationships between water content and emission intensity exhibit excellent linearity, precision and a broad detection range. R2 values span from 0.853 to 0.994, while the LOD is from 0.30 % to 3.2 %. Moreover, the synthesized AIE-CDs powder displays a propensity for strong SSF, which holds potential for the fabrication of WLEDs. The resulting pc-WLEDs device emits a brilliant white light with a CCT of 3876 K, a CRI of 89.5 and a luminous efficacy of 54.01 lm/W. In a word, strong and stable solid-state emission as well as solvent-sensitive solution-state fluorescence are successfully achieved in one carbon dots, which satisfy a variety of application requirements.

Weiying Zhou: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Zishan Sun: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Jiaqi Fan: Investigation, Methodology. Wenjing Huang: Investigation, Methodology. Jiahui Zhang: Investigation, Methodology. Hongji Song: . Liya Zhou: Funding acquisition. Jinqing Huang: . Zhan-chao Wu: Resources. Xinguo Zhang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The research is funded by the Science Foundation of Guangxi Province (No. 2022GXNSFDA080005), Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation (No. ZR2022ME113), Guangzhou Scientific planning program (No. 201804010260), and National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21601081). Jinqing Huang acknowledges the financial support from Science, Technology and Innovation Commission of Shenzhen Municipality (Project number JCYJ20180306174930894)

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.123328.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Solvatochromism of bright carbon dots with tunable long-wavelength emission from green to red and their application as solid-state materials for warm WLEDs

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Lin, C. Lin, M.L. He, R.R. Yuan, Y.J. Zhang, Y.Q. Zhou, W.D. Xiang, X.J. Liang, Solvatochromism of bright carbon dots with tunable long-wavelength emission from green to red and their application as solid-state materials for warm WLEDs, RSC Adv. 7 (2017) 41552-41560.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Excitation wavelength independent visible color emission of carbon dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Wang, C. Sun, X. Chen, Y. Zhang, V.L. Colvin, Q. Rice, J. Seo, S. Feng, S. Wang, W.W. Yu, Excitation wavelength independent visible color emission of carbon dots, Nanoscale 9 (2017) 1909-1915.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel mechanism for red emission carbon dots: hydrogen bond dominated molecular states emission

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T.X. Zhang, J.Y. Zhu, Y. Zhai, H. Wang, X. Bai, B. Dong, H.Y. Wang, H.W. Song, A novel mechanism for red emission carbon dots: hydrogen bond dominated molecular states emission, Nanoscale 9 (2017) 13042-13051.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile synthesis of red-emissive carbon dots with theoretical understanding for cellular imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Wei, D. Yang, L. Wang, Z. Wen, Z. Cui, L. Wang, H. He, W. Zhang, Z. Han, S. Mei, S. Qu, R. Guo, Facile synthesis of red-emissive carbon dots with theoretical understanding for cellular imaging, Colloids and Surfaces B: Biointerfaces 220 (2022) 112869.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Morphology tunable hybrid carbon nanosheets with solvatochromism

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Strongly green-photoluminescent graphene quantum dots for bioimaging applications
